At 8 a.m. EDT on the 26th, the oceanic storm's surface pressure dropped to 964 millibars (it would bottom at 955 millibars later that day), a drop of 43 millibars in a 24 hour period.||This was the last event in which measurable snow occurred at the New Castle County Airport near Wilmington. The entire seasonal snowfall total at the airport was 56.1 inches and was the second snowiest season on record to the 72.8 inches of snow that fell during the 2009-10 season. Snow removal efforts cost the state of Delaware an estimated thirteen million dollars, four times what was budgeted for the snowfall season.

Heavy snow fell across Kent and Sussex Counties in Delaware from the afternoon of the 25th into the early morning of the 26th. This was the last widespread measurable snow event of the winter season to affect the state. Snowfall averaged around one inch in New Castle County and four to seven inches in Kent and Sussex Counties. Because the heaviest snow fell late in the afternoon and at night, the overall number of accidents was less than the previous event. This marked the third time in the month of March that heavy snow fell in Delaware. The snow started during the first half of the afternoon on the 25th, fell heavy at times in the southern half of Delaware during the late afternoon and the first half of the evening of the 25th and ended from north to south during the early morning on the 26th.  In addition, to the heavy snow, gusty northwest winds through the daytime hours on the 26th caused drifting of the snow back onto roadways and hampered snow removal efforts. Peak wind gusts averaged around 40 mph.||Delaware State Police in Kent and Sussex Counties responded to about forty accidents, eight with injuries (five in Sussex County and three in Kent County) and about twenty-five disabled vehicles. Many roadways during the event remained snow covered and icy, especially in Sussex County.  Interstate speed limits were lowered in New Castle County.||Event snowfall totals included 6.2 inches in Georgetown (Sussex County), 5.5 inches in Seaford (Sussex County), 5.2 inches in Harrington (Kent County), 5.0 inches in Delmar (Sussex County), 4.5 inches in Viola (Kent County), 4.0 inches in Woodside and Dover (Kent County), 1.2 inches in Newark (New Castle County) and 0.7 inches at the New Castle County Airport.  ||Delaware was located at the western periphery of the heavy snow shield associated with an intense ocean low pressure system. This low pressure system formed in the Gulf of Mexico along a stationary front on the 24th. It moved northeast and was located east of Savannah, Georgia at 8 a.m. EDT on the 25th. It continued to move northeast at 8 p.m. EDT on the 25th was located well offshore of Cape Hatteras, North Carolina. A second low pressure system then formed closer to the North Carolina coast near Cape Fear. It too moved northeast and intensified rapidly. It made its closest pass to the Delmarva Peninsula very early on the 26th before merging with the first low pressure system. At 8 a.m. EDT on the 26th, the oceanic storm's surface pressure dropped to 964 millibars (it would bottom at 955 millibars later that day), a drop of 43 millibars in a 24 hour period.||This was the last event in which measurable snow occurred at the New Castle County Airport near Wilmington. The entire seasonal snowfall total at the airport was 56.1 inches and was the second snowiest season on record to the 72.8 inches of snow that fell during the 2009-10 season. Snow removal efforts cost the state of Delaware an estimated thirteen million dollars, four times what was budgeted for the snowfall season.

Snow in New Castle County and a wintry mix in Kent and Sussex Counties during the morning of the 18th caused slippery and hazardous traveling conditions on untreated roadways.  Delaware State Police responded to approximately 65 accidents, 14 of them with injuries as well as about 25 disabled vehicles. Most of the disabled vehicles were vehicles that skidded off of roadways. The majority of the accidents were in Kent and New Castle Counties, but the majority of the injuries were in Sussex County.  Snowfall averaged 1 to 2 inches in Kent and New Castle Counties and less than one inch in Sussex County. Ice accumulations were less than one tenth of an inch in Kent and Sussex Counties.  Many school districts had delayed openings as did the city of Wilmington.||In New Castle County, snow fell between 2 a.m. and 7 a.m. EST on the 18th. In Kent and Sussex Counties, the snow started around 2 a.m. EST on the 18th also, but changed briefly to sleet and then freezing rain between 5 a.m. EST and 6 a.m. EST that morning. Precipitation transitioned to plain rain around 6 a.m. EST in Sussex County, but continued to fall as freezing rain in Kent County until it ended around 730 a.m. EST on the 18th.||Representative snowfall totals included 2.3 inches in Talleyville (New Castle County), 2.0 inches at the New Castle County Airport, Newport and Newark (New Castle County), 1.8 inches in Glasgow (New Castle County), 1.6 inches in Dover (Kent County) and 0.5 inches in Bridgeville and Ellendale in Sussex County.||The snow was caused by an occluded front that moved into West Virginia during the early morning of the 18th. A new low pressure system formed on a warm front over the Delmarva Peninsula around sunrise on the 18th and then tracked northeast reaching the central New Jersey coast in the middle of the morning.

Strong gusty west to northwest winds occurred in Delaware during the afternoon and evening of the 27th following an arctic cold frontal passage. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph in Kent and New Castle Counties and around 40 mph in Sussex County.  The strong winds knocked down some weak tree limbs. Peak wind gusts for the day included 46 mph at the Dover Air Force Base (Kent County), 45 mph in Wilmington (New Castle County) and 40 mph at the New Castle County Airport.

Strong to high northeast to east winds associated with the coastal low pressure system occurred across Delaware during the morning of the 13th. While these winds were strong enough to change the snow to rain, they also helped cause minor tidal flooding during the morning high tide cycle in Delaware Bay and along the Atlantic Coast. The winds also tore down weak tree limbs and wires and caused power outages. The combination of the wind and snow caused about 6,900 Delmarva Power and Light customers to lose power, most in New Castle County. ||Peak wind gusts included 58 mph in Lewes and Dewey Beach (Sussex County), 52 mph in Harrington (Kent County), 51 mph in Georgetown (Sussex County), 46 mph at the New Castle County Airport, Newark (New Castle County) and Indian River (Sussex County) and 44 mph in Dover (Kent County). The highest tide reached 6.89 feet above mean lower low water at Breakwater Harbor (Sussex County). Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.0 feet above mean lower low water.  Moderate tidal flooding starts at 7.0 feet above mean lower low water.||The strong winds and the subsequent trigger for minor tidal flooding were caused by a low pressure system that moved northeast along the seaboard on the 13th. The strongest pressure gradient (difference) between the approaching low pressure system and a departing high pressure system south of the Canadian Maritimes was maximized during the morning of the 13th.

Some spotty freezing drizzle occurred in Sussex County during the morning of the 5th. Untreated roadways and walkways were slippery. Temperatures rose above freezing during the second half of the morning. The freezing drizzle was caused by a moist onshore flow circulating around a weak low pressure system well east of the Virginia coastline.

Some spotty freezing drizzle occurred in Sussex County during the morning of the 5th. Untreated roadways and walkways were slippery. Temperatures rose above freezing during the second half of the morning. The freezing drizzle was caused by a moist onshore flow circulating around a weak low pressure system well east of the Virginia coastline.

The strong pressure gradient (difference) between an intensifying strong low pressure system and a high pressure system in the Ohio Valley caused strong to high northwest winds to occur across Delaware from the evening of the 12th following a cold frontal passage through the daytime hours on the 13th. The highest winds occurred overnight on the 12th.  Peak wind gusts averaged 50 to 60 mph and knocked down weak tree limbs, trees and power lines. Scattered power outages occurred and affected the city of Wilmington. A pole fire occurred in Elsmere (New Castle County). Peak wind gusts included 62 mph in Lewes (Sussex County), 55 mph in Dewey Beach (Sussex County), 54 mph in Milton (Sussex County), 53 mph at the New Castle County Airport, 50 mph in Bellevue (New Castle County), 49 mph in Harrington (Kent County) and Dover (Kent County), 48 mph in Ellendale (Sussex County) and 46 mph in Newark (New Castle County).||The low pressure system responsible for the strong winds emerged from the Rockies on the evening of the 10th. It reached central Missouri on the evening of the 11th and western Pennsylvania on the afternoon of the 12th. It already was an intense 984 millibar low pressure system. The northeast moving 982 millibar low pressure system reached central New Jersey at 8 p.m. EDT on the 12th as its cold front was moving through Delaware. The 981 millibar low pressure system was near Boston, Massachusetts at 11 p.m. EDT on the 12th and reached Nova Scotia as a 976 millibar low pressure system at 5 a.m. EDT on the 13th. It continued to move northeast through the Canadian Maritimes during the day on the 13th and very slowly weakened. By evening on the 13th, the high pressure system was close enough to Delaware that the pressure gradient (difference) weakened and the strong winds stopped.

A slow moving cold front with waves of low pressure along it brought heavy rain to Delaware on the 16th. This caused poor drainage flooding as well as minor flooding in the Christina River Basin. Rain began during the early morning of the 16th and fell heavy at times throughout the event. It ended during the late afternoon with event totals of around two inches in New Castle County. Rainfall amounts in Kent and Sussex Counties averaged 1 to 2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Christina River at Coochs Bridge was above its 10.5 foot flood stage from 107 p.m. EDT through 427 p.m. EDT on the 16th. It crested at 10.91 feet at 3 p.m. EDT.  Event precipitation totals included 2.14 inches in Glasgow, 2.03 inches in White Clay Creek, 1.90 inches in Odessa, 1.89 inches in Hockessin and Glasgow, 1.85 inches in Newark, 1.74 inches in Middletown and 1.31 inches at the New Castle County Airport.

A very strong cold front and strong winds above the ground helped produce supercell type thunderstorms across Delaware during the second half of the afternoon on the 22nd. One confirmed tornado, large and destructive hail and pockets of wind damage occurred throughout the state. The confirmed tornado with two injuries occurred in West Dover Hundred in Kent County. Over 3,000 homes and businesses lost power throughout the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado with an estimated maximum wind speed of 105 mph touched down in West Dover Hundred.  It touched down just west of Darling Drive and lifted just east of Darling Farm Road.  Two people were injured as the worst wind damage occurred on Darling Drive.  A 58-year-old man and his wife were injured after the tornado elevated, spun and then leveled their mobile home. The man had a bureau, a file cabinet and a mattress over him. The tornado damaged about seventeen homes in the area, leaving four condemned and uninhabitable. The tornado also snapped five poles. A couple of  thousand homes and businesses were also without power. The tornado was embedded within a swath of other damaging winds. The estimated path length was 0.35 miles and the estimated maximum path width was 50 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds followed a strong cold frontal passage in Delaware starting on the afternoon of the 15th. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph and coupled with the rain knocked down some weak tree limbs and wires and caused isolated power outages. While the strongest winds occurred during the second half of the afternoon on the 15th in interior Delaware, strong northwest winds persisted through the night of the 15th along Delaware Bay.   Peak wind gusts included 49 mph at the New Castle County Airport, 46 mph in Lewes (Sussex County) and 44 mph in Smyrna (Kent County).  The strong gusty winds were caused by the pressure gradient (difference) between the cold front and the approaching high pressure system over the lower Ohio Valley. When the high pressure system reached the upper Ohio Valley on the morning of the 16th, the strong winds ceased.

A nearly stationary frontal boundary focused slow moving thunderstorms with heavy rain in New Castle County during the afternoon of the 10th. This caused flash flooding in the county. Doppler Radar storm total estimates exceeded three inches in north central New Castle County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain led to flash flooding of roadways around the intersection of Interstate 95 and Churchmans Road in the northeast part of White Clay Hundred.  The left lanes of Interstate 95 in both directions were flooded. Southbound Interstate 95 was down to one lane open. Southbound Delaware State Route 1 was closed at Interstate 95 due to flooding. The ramps from Churchmans Road were also flooded.  Sand and mud runoff spilled onto the Delaware State Route 1 ramp from the Christina Mall. The heavy rain also caused a gas station's canopy collapse at University Plaza in Christina.  Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached between 3 and 4 inches in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot start to July peaked on the 2nd with high temperatures well into the 90s and afternoon heat index values reaching near 105 degrees. An approaching cold front which started to trigger a series of showers and thunderstorms made the 3rd slightly cooler.  The passage of Hurricane Arthur to the east on Independence Day ended the run of 90 degree weather.  Highest temperatures included 98 degrees in Georgetown (Sussex County), 97 degrees in Kitts Hummock (Kent County), 96 degrees in Dover and Smyrna (Kent County), 95 degrees in the White Clay Creek Park and Glasgow in New Castle County and Ellendale and Milton in Sussex County and 92 degrees at the New Castle County Airport and Millsboro (Sussex County).

The first back-to-back days with high temperatures of 90 degrees or higher occurred across Delaware on the 17th and 18th. High temperatures were in the lower to mid 90s both days with the hottest weather on the 18th. Afternoon heat index values were in the mid 90s on the 17th and the upper 90s on the 18th. A 56-year old man from Sussex County was the state���s first heat-related death of the year. Highest temperatures included 97 degrees in Georgetown (Sussex County), Rehoboth Beach (Sussex County) and Ellendale (Sussex County), 96 degrees in Redden (Sussex County), 94 degrees in Dover (Kent County) and 93 degrees in Smyrna (Kent County),  the New Castle County Airport and Glasgow (New Castle County). A slow moving cold front that sagged south through Delaware on the 19th ended the heat.

A slow moving low pressure system and a deep southerly flow from the Gulf of Mexico and then the Atlantic Ocean dropped heavy rain across Delaware centered on the 30th. Event precipitation totals averaged 2 to 5 inches, with the highest amounts in New Castle County. This caused poor drainage and field flooding as well as flooding of creeks and rivers, mainly in New Castle County. Creek flooding in parts of New Castle County were the highest since Tropical Storm Irene in August of 2011. The steady rain began during the afternoon of the 29th and fell at its heaviest from the morning into the early evening of the 30th. The rain ended during the late evening of the 30th.|  |The heavy rain was caused by the combination of a strong high pressure system that built over the Canadian Maritimes that initiated the onshore flow and a strong, but slow moving low pressure system. The low pressure system was located in northwest Missouri on the morning of the 29th. It moved northeast to near Chicago during the afternoon of the 29th. The low pressure system then became nearly stationary in southern Wisconsin as its cold front reached Ohio early on the 30th. Then a new low pressure system developed on the frontal boundary and was over Eastern Tennessee on the morning of the 30th. The low pressure system reached West Virginia during the late afternoon on the 30th as its warm front started to move through Delmarva. The low pressure system reached north central Maryland at 11 p.m. EDT on the 30th as its warm front reached extreme southeast Pennsylvania and central New Jersey. The low pressure system reached the Allentown, Pennsylvania area at 5 a.m. EDT on May 1st and then was absorbed again into the frontal boundary associated with the original low as it moved into New York State that morning. The steady rain ended as the low pressure system passed north of Delaware.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused considerable poor drainage and major river and creek flooding in New Castle County. Some of the crests were the highest since Tropical Storm Irene on August 28, 2011. Within Wilmington alone, five roadways were flooded and closed. The Christina River at Coochs Bridge had major flooding and was above its 10.5 foot flood stage from 124 p.m. EDT on the 30th through 332 a.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 12.67 feet at 1015 p.m. EDT on the 30th. This was historically the 6th highest crest on record for the river.  The White Clay Creek at Newark had minor flooding and was above its 11.5 foot flood stage from 744 p.m. EDT on the 30th through 334 a.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 13.71 feet at 1100 p.m. EDT on the 30th. Farther downstream, the White Clay Creek at Delaware Park had major flooding and was above its 13 foot flood stage from 413 p.m. EDT on the 30th through 802 a.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 16.90 feet at 100 a.m. EDT on May 1st. This was historically the 6th highest crest on record for the creek and the highest crest since August 28, 2011 associated with Tropical Storm Irene.  The Red Clay Creek at Wooddale had major flooding and was above its 7 foot flood stage from 417 p.m. EDT on the 30th through 403 a.m. EDT on the 1st. It crested at 11.41 feet at 1015 p.m. EDT on the 30th.  It was the 5th highest crest on record and the highest crest since Tropical Storm Irene on August 28, 2011.  Farther downstream, the Red Clay Creek at Stanton crested just below major flooding criteria. It was above its 15 foot flood stage from 407  p.m. EDT on the 30th through 627 a.m. EDT on the 1st. It crested at 19.97 feet at 1145 p.m. EDT on the 30th.  It was the 5th highest crest on record and also the highest crest since Tropical Storm Irene on August 28, 2011.  The Brandywine Creek at Wilmington had major flooding and was above its 16.5 foot flood stage from 1033 p.m. EDT on the 30th through 738 p.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 20.70 feet at 6 a.m. EDT on the 1st, a new record.  This new gage has been at this location since 2007. Higher relative crests could have occurred prior to that year.  Event precipitation totals included 5.15 inches in Prices Corner, 5.10 inches in Newark, 4.76 inches in Newport, 4.60 inches at the Wilmington Airport and 4.50 inches in Odessa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening and overnight high tide cycle on April 30th, low end minor tidal flooding occurred in Sussex County, but the tidal flooding became progressively worse farther up Delaware Bay and into the tidal Delaware River. Along the tidal Delaware River in New Castle County, moderate tidal flooding occurred as freshwater runoff exacerbated the flooding. The highest tide for the event at Breakwater Harbor (Lewes, Sussex County) was 6.91 feet above mean lower low water on the evening of April 29th. Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.0 feet above mean lower low water. The highest tide at Reedy Island (New Castle County) reached 8.25 feet above mean lower low water at 1254 a.m. EDT on May 1st. Moderate tidal flooding starts at 8.2 feet above mean lower low water.||The tidal flooding was caused by the combination of a strong high pressure system that built over the Canadian Maritimes that initiated the onshore flow and a strong, but slow moving low pressure system. The low pressure system was located in northwest Missouri on the morning of April 29th. It moved northeast to near Chicago during the afternoon of April 29th. The low pressure system then became nearly stationary in southern Wisconsin as its cold front reached Ohio early on April 30th. Then a new low pressure system developed on the frontal boundary and was over Eastern Tennessee on the morning of April 30th. The low pressure system reached West Virginia during the late afternoon on April 30th as its warm front started to move through Delmarva. The low pressure system reached north central Maryland at 11 p.m. EDT on April 30th as its warm front reached extreme southeast Pennsylvania and central New Jersey. The low pressure system reached the Allentown, Pennsylvania area at 5 a.m. EDT on May 1st and then was absorbed again into the frontal boundary associated with the original low as it moved into New York State that morning. The steady rain ended as the low pressure system passed north of the Eastern Shore. But the formation of the second low pressure system and the passage of the warm front helped veer the winds from the east to the southeast and enhanced the surface flow up the Delaware Bay that produced the most tidal flooding.

A very strong cold front and strong winds above the ground helped produce supercell type thunderstorms across Delaware during the second half of the afternoon on the 22nd. One confirmed tornado, large and destructive hail and pockets of wind damage occurred throughout the state. The confirmed tornado with two injuries occurred in West Dover Hundred in Kent County. Over 3,000 homes and businesses lost power throughout the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped hail as large as tennis balls in the Ballymeade development of Brandywine Hundred. Siding and vehicles were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front triggered thunderstorms with heavy downpours across Delaware.  Localized amounts reached around two inches and caused flash flooding during the late afternoon and evening of the 13th. In addition, the combination of the preceding onshore flow and spring tides associated with the full moon caused minor tidal flooding during the evening high tide cycle on the 13th. This was the third of four successive evenings with minor tidal flooding. The highest tide at Lewes (Sussex County) was 6.24 feet above mean lower low water on the evening of the 11th. Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.00 feet above mean lower low water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy downpours caused flash flooding in the Millsboro and Long Neck areas of Sussex County around Indian River Bay. The flash flooding was enhanced because the heavy rain and run-off coincided with the high tide.  Event precipitation totals included 1.90 inches in Millsboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system that developed along a frontal boundary helped produce clusters of showers and some strong thunderstorms that moved through Delaware during the early morning of the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike started a house fire in Hockessin (Mill Creek Hundred). No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening approaching cold front triggered a series of strong to severe thunderstorms that affected Delaware during the evening and overnight on the 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees and wires in Cheswold.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening approaching cold front triggered a series of strong to severe thunderstorms that affected Delaware during the evening and overnight on the 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore siding from homes, knocked down trees and mail boxes in Smyrna.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening approaching cold front triggered a series of strong to severe thunderstorms that affected Delaware during the evening and overnight on the 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm uprooted several trees and knocked down numerous branches in Bear.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening approaching cold front triggered a series of strong to severe thunderstorms that affected Delaware during the evening and overnight on the 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down several trees and wires in Marydel.  One downed tree badly damaged an outdoor grill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening approaching cold front triggered a series of strong to severe thunderstorms that affected Delaware during the evening and overnight on the 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down numerous trees in Dover.  Just west of Dover, a wind gust of 51 mph was measured in Voshell Cove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening approaching cold front triggered a series of strong to severe thunderstorms that affected Delaware during the evening and overnight on the 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down several trees and wires in Hartly. One tree fell onto and damaged a building. Another downed tree damaged a house's porch. A third large downed tree took down wires along Crystal Road.

A few buses were also surrounded by flood waters within the city.  Event precipitation totals included 1.78 inches in Dover.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped trigger a line of thunderstorms that moved through Delaware during the afternoon of the 21st. Isolated wind damage occurred with one severe thunderstorm in Kent County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down large tree limbs in Dover, one of which punctured the roof of the Central Delaware Habitat for Humanity ReStore building. Rain water leaked into the building and damaged the rear of the store. No injuries were reported. A wind gust to 47 mph occurred at the Dover Air Force Base.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing cold front triggered snow showers in Delaware and in Kent and New Castle Counties in particular during the morning into the early afternoon of the 26th. Snowfall in Kent and New Castle Counties averaged 1 to 2 inches and less than 1 inch in Sussex County.  The snow showers started between 5 a.m. and 6 a.m. EST on the 26th, fell at their heaviest between 10 a.m. EST and 11 a.m. EST that morning and finished moving through between Noon and 2 p.m. EST that afternoon. During the heavier snow bursts, untreated and less traveled roadways became snow covered and slippery. Delaware State Police responded to about 40 traffic accidents, nearly all of them in New Castle County.||Representative snowfall included 2.1 inches in Mount Cuba (New Castle County), 2.0 inches in Greenville (New Castle County) and Harrington (Kent County), 1.6 inches in Claymont (New Castle County), 1.4 inches in Newark (New Castle County), 1.1 inches in Dover (Kent County), Hockessin (New Castle County) and 0.9 inches in Laurel (Sussex County).||A cold front developed over Western Pennsylvania early on the 26th and progressed southeast. It moved through Delaware from the northwest to southeast between 11 a.m. EST and 2 p.m. EST on the 26th.

The very persistent and strong west to northwest winds that started during the afternoon of the 27th coupled with the greater tidal fluctuations associated with the spring tides with the approaching new moon caused blowout tides during the morning low tide cycle on the 28th along tidal sections of the Delaware River in Delaware. At Reedy Point, the low tide reached 1.88 feet below mean lower low water. At Delaware City, the low tide reached 1.93 feet below mean lower low water. Blowout tides start at 1.80 feet below mean lower low water. The northwest winds decreased during the day on the 28th and while tidal levels were below normal astronomical levels, no further blowout conditions occurred.

A low pressure system that formed along a stationary front across the southeastern United States was able to spread snow into Delaware during the daytime on the 25th. Snowfall averaged around one inch in Kent and Sussex Counties. Little if any snow accumulated in New Castle County as the intensity was lighter. The snow fell from around 7 a.m. EST to around 4 p.m. EST and fell at its heaviest around Noon EST. Less traveled and untreated roadways were slippery. Representative snowfall included 1.5 inches in Harrington (Kent County) and 0.8 inches in Georgetown (Sussex County) and Dover (Kent County).  The low pressure system responsible for the snow formed in Georgia along a stationary front around 7 a.m. EST on the 25th. The low pressure system reached South Carolina at 10 a.m. EST that morning and moved offshore near Cape Hatteras, North Carolina around 3 p.m. EST that afternoon near the time that the snow was ending.

Snow fell across Delaware during the late afternoon and early evening of the 9th, but had its greatest and longest impact in New Castle County. Snowfall averaged around 2 inches in New Castle County and less than half an inch throughout the rest of Delaware. The snow made for slippery travel, especially in New Castle County. Delaware State Police reported approzimately 30 snow related accidents, 4 with injuries and about 20 disabled vehicles.  Precipitation began falling between 4 p.m. and 7 p.m. EST on the 9th, starting in Sussex County the latest. It started briefly as sleet or mixed with sleet before becoming snow. The snow fell at its heaviest around 6 p.m. EST in New Castle County. The snow lasted the shortest time in Sussex County and continued deeper into the evening farther north in the state. The snow ended between 7 p.m. EST and 930 p.m. EST that evening.  ||Representative snowfall included 2.2 inches in Wilmington (New Castle County), 2.0 inches at the New Castle County Airport and Newark (New Castle County), 1.8 inches in Glasgow (New Castle County) and Greenville (New Castle County), 1.3 inches in Townsend (New Castle County) and 0.3 inches in Dover (Kent County). The snow was caused by an occluded front that reached West Virginia during the early afternoon of the 9th and passed through Delaware during the first half of the evening on the 9th.

Strong northwest winds behind the departing strong low pressure system coupled with another arctic air mass dropped low temperatures on the morning of the 22nd into the single numbers in Delaware and produced wind chill factors as low as 10 degrees to 20 degrees below zero.  In many places, low temperatures were as cold as January 7th and wind chill factors came close to matching that morning.  ||Lowest hourly wind chills included 18 degree below zero at the New Castle County Airport, 17 degrees below zero in Dover (Kent County) and 9 degrees below zero in Georgetown (Sussex County). ||Actual low temperatures included 3 degrees above zero at the New Castle County Airport, matching the low on January 7th for the coldest day of the winter. The calendar day low temperature in Georgetown was 5 degrees above zero. This also matched the low on January 7th. The lowest reading of the winter season there occurred on January 30th (5 below zero). Other low temperatures that morning included 1 degree above zero in Newark (New Castle County), 2 degrees above zero in Glasgow (New Castle County), 3 degrees above zero in Smyrna (Kent County), 4 degrees above zero in Dover (Kent County), 5 degrees above zero in Laurel (Sussex County) and 6 degrees above zero in Delmar (Sussex County).

The combination of a relatively moist air mass and light winds preceding a warm front produced widespread dense fog in Delaware on the 21st. In Kent and Sussex Counties, the dense fog occurred in the morning. In New Castle County, the presence of snow cover helped the dense fog persist longer until around Noon EST. The fog lifted and dispersed as a warm front moved north of Delaware.
